Porte Saint-Antoine was a principal eastern gate of medieval Paris, integral to the city's fortifications and serving as a vital entry for commerce and defense from the late 12th century until its demolition in the late 18th  ...    ## Location and Context

### Geographical Position in Paris The Porte Saint-Antoine occupied a strategic position on the eastern boundary of medieval Paris, marking the endpoint of the rue Saint-Antoine as it exited the city walls toward the faubourg Saint-Antoine.[4] This gate was situated  ...    The site of Porte Saint-Antoine lay along an ancient Roman road constructed to connect Lutetia (Paris) with eastern towns including Meaux and Melun, serving as a key thoroughfare for trade and travel from antiquity.[8] In the late 12th  ...    flows.[5][24]  The site of the Porte Saint-Antoine now lies at the eastern terminus of the rue Saint-Antoine, integrated into the Place de la Bastille in Paris's 4th arrondissement, near the intersection of the Boulevard
